answers.question: List of scenarios
answer: Scenario 1: Orderly Decarbonisation (SSP1, RCP1.9 and 2.6, 1.5°C warming by 2100)
Scenario 2: Regional Rivalry (SSP3, RCP4.5, 2.9°C warming by 2100)
answers.evidence: T&G's climate scenarios: SCENARIO 1: ORDERLY DECARBONISATION SSP1 RCP1.9 and 2.6 — 1.5°C warming by 2100 ... SCENARIO 2: REGIONAL RIVALRY SSP3 RCP4.5 — 2.9°C warming by 2100 ... SCENARIO 3: HOTHOUSE SSP5 RCP8.5 — 4.8°C warming by 2100
answers.notes: Three temperature-aligned scenarios developed in 2023 and unchanged in 2024.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: 1.5°C warming by 2100 (Scenario 1: Orderly Decarbonisation, SSP1/RCP1.9 and 2.6); 2.9°C warming by 2100 (Scenario 2: Regional Rivalry, SSP3/RCP4.5); and 4.8°C warming by 2100 (Scenario 3: Hothouse, SSP5/RCP8.5). All are expressed as rises in average global temperatures in the 2081–2100 period relative to the pre-industrial baseline (1850–1900). No temperature overshoot is mentioned.
answers.evidence: SCENARIO 1: ORDERLY DECARBONISATION SSP1 RCP1.9 and 2.6 — 1.5°C warming by 2100 ... SCENARIO 2: REGIONAL RIVALRY SSP3 RCP4.5 — 2.9°C warming by 2100 ... SCENARIO 3: HOTHOUSE SSP5 RCP8.5 — 4.8°C warming by 2100 [footnote: Rise in average global temperatures in the 2081-2100 period relative to the pre-industrial baseline (1850-1900)]
answers.notes: No mention of temperature overshoot in the document.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple providers are named: (1) The Aotearoa Circle (Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios, developed 2022–2023); (2) IPCC (RCP and SSP frameworks); (3) Aurecon Limited (Asia Pacific design, engineering and advisory company that worked with T&G to develop the three temperature-aligned scenarios); and (4) NZ CS 1 (Aotearoa New Zealand Climate Standard) which mandated certain scenarios.
answers.evidence: The establishment of T&G's three scenarios was guided by the requirements of the NZ CS 1 and The Aotearoa Circle's Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios. Sector scenarios were developed by The Aotearoa Circle in 2022 and 2023 by bringing together the diversity of the agriculture sector to collaborate, share knowledge, science and insights, and inform the outcome. T&G participated as a member of the Technical Expert Group. In line with the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), T&G has used both Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) and Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSPs) as the basis of our climate scenarios. ... T&G worked with Aurecon Limited, an Asia Pacific design, engineering and advisory company to develop three temperature-aligned climate scenarios.
answers.notes: Aurecon Limited developed the scenarios with T&G; The Aotearoa Circle provided the sector-level scenarios that guided them; IPCC provided the underlying RCP/SSP frameworks.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: SCENARIO 1: ORDERLY DECARBONISATION SSP1 RCP1.9 and 2.6 ... SCENARIO 2: REGIONAL RIVALRY SSP3 RCP4.5 ... SCENARIO 3: HOTHOUSE SSP5 RCP8.5. Regional rivalry (2.9°C), our second scenario, differs from the sector scenarios. It was selected as we consider regional rivalry (a combination of SSP3 and RCP4.5) to be the more comprehensive challenge for our business.
answers.location_hint: Table 1 and Rationale for scenarios, p.10-11
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both SSP and RCP identifiers for each scenario. SSP1 is paired with RCP1.9/2.6, SSP3 with RCP4.5, and SSP5 with RCP8.5. SSP1+RCP1.9 and SSP5+RCP8.5 are broadly consistent pairings, but SSP3+RCP4.5 is a cross-family combination. The document explicitly acknowledges this as a deliberate choice.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: The establishment of T&G's three scenarios was guided by the requirements of the NZ CS 1 and The Aotearoa Circle's Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios. Sector scenarios were developed by The Aotearoa Circle in 2022 and 2023 by bringing together the diversity of the agriculture sector to collaborate, share knowledge, science and insights, and inform the outcome. T&G participated as a member of the Technical Expert Group. ... Regional rivalry (2.9°C), our second scenario, differs from the sector scenarios. It was selected as we consider regional rivalry (a combination of SSP3 and RCP4.5) to be the more comprehensive challenge for our business given the aspects of deglobalisation, increased national food security and the subsequent effects on consumer preferences and market size.
answers.notes: The scenarios were adapted for the New Zealand agriculture/horticulture sector context, with T&G participating in the Technical Expert Group that developed the Aotearoa Circle sector scenarios. T&G further customised by selecting a different second scenario (Regional Rivalry) that better reflected its specific business challenges. The scenarios were developed with Aurecon Limited specifically for T&G's context.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: Sector scenarios were developed by The Aotearoa Circle in 2022 and 2023 by bringing together the diversity of the agriculture sector to collaborate, share knowledge, science and insights, and inform the outcome. T&G participated as a member of the Technical Expert Group.
answers.notes: The Aotearoa Circle's Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios were developed collaboratively with diverse agriculture sector participants. T&G was a member of the Technical Expert Group. This constitutes participatory/collaborative scenario development at the sector level, which then informed T&G's company-level scenarios.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: No explicit baseline or reference scenario is described. However, the document references a pre-industrial baseline (1850–1900) for temperature measurements, and financial impact modelling uses 2040 under Scenario 3 (Hothouse) as the reference point for risk assessment.
answers.evidence: Rise in average global temperatures in the 2081-2100 period relative to the pre-industrial baseline (1850-1900). ... The risk impact would occur in 2040, and we are living in scenario 3 — Hothouse. 2040 was chosen as it is a medium-term time horizon for which there is supporting data for climate and our business, such as apple volumes and growing regions. Modelling was not undertaken for the three different scenarios — we do not anticipate significant differences in the financial impacts based on the scenarios until at least 2050.
answers.notes: No formal "baseline scenario" (e.g. a business-as-usual or current-policies scenario) is explicitly described as a comparator against which the three climate scenarios are assessed. The pre-industrial baseline is used only for temperature measurement. The Hothouse scenario is used as the worst-case reference for financial modelling.

answers.question: Model used
answers.answer: No specific named climate models, IAMs, or macroeconomic models are identified in the document. The document references the IPCC's RCP and SSP frameworks, and mentions an internal proprietary financial model developed by T&G, but no named models (e.g. CLIMADA, REMIND, GCAM) are cited.
answers.evidence: In line with the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), T&G has used both Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) and Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSPs) as the basis of our climate scenarios. ... An internal proprietary model to measure the financial impacts of climate risks across key business metrics has also been developed.
answers.notes: No specific named climate or IAM models are mentioned. Only the IPCC RCP/SSP frameworks and an unnamed internal proprietary financial model are referenced.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Overarching risk 1: Damage to T&G operations due to increasing intensity and frequency of severe weather events. R13: Increasing intensity and frequency of flooding events (fluvial/pluvial/coastal storm surges) damages T&G's apple orchards, leading to reduced crop yields. In 2024, the financial impact of this was in the range of $1.0 - $2.0 million. R14: Extreme flooding events damage T&G-owned packhouses, cool stores, inventory, and plant and machinery. R3.2: Increased intensity and frequency of extreme weather events reduces crop yields from T&G's independent growers — in 2024, the financial impact of this was in the range of $3.0 - $4.0 million.
answers.notes: The document explicitly includes acute physical risks (flooding, cyclones, hail, extreme weather events) with financial impact estimates. Cyclone Gabrielle is referenced as a real-world example. Both growing operations and post-harvest facilities are assessed for acute physical risk damage.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: Physical risks emanating from climate change can be event-driven (acute), such as increased severity of extreme weather events. They can also relate to longer-term shifts (chronic) in precipitation and temperature and increased variability in weather patterns. ... Increases in drought frequency and severity, fire and severe weather all have adverse impacts on growers. ... The severity and frequency of ex-tropical cyclones, flooding, drought and fire increases in Aotearoa New Zealand. ... R17: Not enough heat and sunshine hours (growing degree days) during the critical cell ... T&G has an internal carbon price of $89.50/tCO₂e.
answers.location_hint: Scenario narratives p.12-13; risk tables p.25-29; metrics p.33
answers.notes: Many of these variables appear in the scenario narratives and risk tables rather than as explicit quantified scenario drivers. The internal carbon price is used as a metric/tool rather than a scenario variable per se.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: The wider scenarios, however, extend to 2100 (whereas T&G's are to 2073). ... T&G's first scenario, orderly decarbonisation (1.5°C), and third scenario, hothouse (4.8°C), align with both the mandated NZ CS 1 scenarios as well as The Aotearoa Circle sector scenarios. ... The severity and frequency of ex-tropical cyclones, flooding, drought and fire increases in Aotearoa New Zealand, which creates catastrophic growing damage. ... T&G defines the time horizons to assess climate-related risks and opportunities in accordance with the United Nations' Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), NIWA, External Reporting Board (XRB) and ISO14091.
answers.notes: The scenarios are based on global IPCC RCP/SSP frameworks (global scale), but are applied to Aotearoa New Zealand and specific growing regions (e.g. Hawke's Bay) through the Aotearoa Circle sector scenarios and NIWA data. The document does not explicitly describe a formal downscaling methodology, but the application is clearly regional/local.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: 1–8 years; Medium-term: 8–10 years; Long-term: 10–50 years (to 2073). The wider underlying scenarios extend to 2100. Rationales are provided for each timeframe.
answers.evidence: T&G defines the time horizons to assess climate-related risks and opportunities in accordance with the United Nations' Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), NIWA, External Reporting Board (XRB) and ISO14091. Short-term: 1-8 years — This aligns to business planning [and] capital allocation processes. Medium-term: 8-10 years — [aligns] to business planning as well as global climate change ambitions under the Paris [Agreement]. Long-term: 10-50 years — [aligns] to apple orchard growing cycles of 25 years, the life span of major assets and mid/end century time horizons recognised by both IPCC and NIWA. This analysis looked at the next 50 years (to 2073) which aligns with the lifetime of T&G's assets and Aotearoa New Zealand's regulatory aspirations for net zero by 2050. The wider scenarios, however, extend to 2100.
answers.notes: Justification is explicitly provided for each time horizon, referencing business planning cycles, Paris Agreement ambitions, apple orchard growing cycles (25 years), asset lifespans, IPCC/NIWA mid/end-century horizons, and NZ net zero by 2050 aspirations.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: T&G's first scenario, orderly decarbonisation (1.5°C), and third scenario, hothouse (4.8°C), align with both the mandated NZ CS 1 scenarios as well as The Aotearoa Circle sector scenarios. Regional rivalry (2.9°C), our second scenario, differs from the sector scenarios. It was selected as we consider regional rivalry (a combination of SSP3 and RCP4.5) to be the more comprehensive challenge for our business given the aspects of deglobalisation, increased national food security and the subsequent effects on consumer preferences and market size. We selected RCP4.5 because: It provides an intermediate warming scenario from a physical risk perspective (distinctly different from orderly decarbonisation and hothouse scenarios). Of historic availability of RCP4.5 data relative to RCP6.0 data in 2023 when the work was conducted. It is commonly adopted by other reporting entities, allowing for easier comparison of Climate-related Disclosures. It was used in The Aotearoa Circle's sector scenarios, allowing consistency in the translation of sector-to-company specific scenarios. [Scenario 1] Selected to reflect a plausible future in which T&G has to rapidly decarbonise and transition its operations. [Scenario 2] Selected as it poses high levels of disruption to international trade that would affect T&G's business model. [Scenario 3] Selected due to the high level of physical impact that would manifest in T&G's growing operations.
answers.notes: Explicit justification is provided for each scenario choice and for the specific RCP4.5 selection, including regulatory alignment (NZ CS 1), sector consistency (Aotearoa Circle), data availability, comparability with peers, and business-specific relevance.
